experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are Evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes a known evil player, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending an evil on quest 1. Rejecting is standard and does not reveal too much because many good players would also oppose a suspicious round-1 team containing 0.
Player 4: No role-specific information yet beyond my own identity as Good; on round 1 with no quest history, team [0,2] is a standard 2-person opener and rejecting without evidence gives little benefit. As a Servant, approving reasonable early teams helps gather information from the quest result while my vote reveals little beyond being open to a normal first mission.
Player 1: I know Player 0 is evil with me; others are unknown. The proposed team [0,2] includes one evil and one unknown, which is good for evil because it can put an evil player on Quest 1. As Minion in the team voting phase, I want to maximize chances an evil-inclusive team goes through while keeping my vote aligned with a plausible good perspective. Approving helps the evil side and does not reveal much since approving an early team with limited info is common.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2, 3, and 4 are unknown good roles. The current phase is team voting on quest 1 for team [0,2]. As evil, having myself on the team gives us a chance to fail the quest immediately, and on quest 1 a single fail is enough. Approving also looks natural since I’m on the team and doesn’t reveal much beyond mild self-interest.
Player 2: I only know my own role as Good Servant; no reliable reads yet on others since this is round 1 and I proposed this team myself. In a 5-player game, quest 1 only needs 2 players and a single fail to fail, so an opening team including myself is reasonable. As Good, I want an early approved team that contains me so I can guarantee a pass from my seat and generate information from quest outcome. Approving my own opening team reveals little beyond consistency and helps Good by getting an early clean data point rather than stalling.

Passing reveals less and preserves cover for later quests and assassination endgame.
Player 2: I am a Servant on the first quest team with players 0 and 2. I have no confirmed role information yet; only slight signal is player 3 rejected this team while others approved, but that's far from conclusive. In quest voting as Good, optimal play is always to pass since failing is only for Evil and an unexpected fail would hurt Good and falsely implicate the team. My pass reveals consistency with being Good and does not give Evil any useful extra information beyond normal gameplay.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil, so team [2,3,4] excludes all known evil and is maximally strong for Good. The phase is team voting, and approving a clean team is optimal. Approving also subtly encourages a full-good bloc without revealing too much, since rejecting would be inconsistent with my knowledge and could hinder Good progress.
Player 4: Quest 1 succeeded with team [2,0], so 2 and 0 have some positive evidence but either could still be evil who chose to pass. Proposed team [2,3,4] includes me and keeps 2 from the successful team while adding 3. As a good Servant, on team voting I should approve teams that are reasonably likely good and avoid needless rejections early. This vote reveals mild trust in 2 and willingness to test 3 while including myself, which is acceptable for good.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2, 3, 4 are nominally good from my perspective. Quest 1 succeeded on team [2,0], meaning I chose not to fail and 2 is likely trusted now. Current phase is team voting. The proposed team [2,3,4] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and give Good another point. As evil, optimal play is to reject strong all-good teams when possible. My reject reveals little because evil commonly reject non-evil teams, but a single reject among others is not too informative.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate and I am evil. Quest 1 succeeded on team [2,0], which means 0 chose to pass or was hiding; that gives good players some trust in 2 and possibly 0. Current team [2,3,4] excludes both evils, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and likely to succeed, giving good another point. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject strong all-good teams, especially ones with no evil presence. My reject also weakly signals suspicion of a clean team, but that's acceptable since preventing/contesting good teams helps evil more than blending in here.
Player 2: I only know Quest 1 with team [2,0] succeeded, so players 2 and 0 look somewhat good but success on a 2-person quest is not conclusive. Proposed team [2,3,4] keeps me on the team and adds two unknowns while excluding 0, who is the only player with any positive evidence. As a Good Servant in team voting, I should prefer teams with stronger evidence and reject low-information teams early when there is little penalty. Rejecting signals caution and preference for including proven members, which helps Good more than approving a mostly untested team.

Player 4: I am Good Servant. Team [0,2] previously succeeded on quest 1, but quest 2 with [0,2,4] failed, and since I know I am good, at least one of 0 or 2 is evil. This 2-person team must therefore include an evil player and is very risky to send on quest 3. Optimal good play is to reject and seek teams excluding one of them for more information. My reject signals suspicion on the 0/2 pair, which helps Good.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 [2,0] succeeded with 0 fails, which makes 2 look good because I chose to pass. Voting approve is optimal. My approval is consistent with a leader backing own team and does not reveal extra information beyond expected self-interest.
Player 2: I know I am Good. Player 0 and I were on the first successful 2-person quest, which makes 0 somewhat more trustworthy. The failed 3-person quest was [0,2,4]; since I am Good, exactly one of 0 or 4 is Evil. Combining that with quest 1 success slightly favors 0 over 4, so [0,2] is currently one of the strongest available pairs. In team voting as Good, I should approve teams that maximize success chances and preserve useful signal. Approving [0,2] tests the hypothesis that 0 is Good; if it succeeds, 0 is strongly cleared, and if it fails, 0 is exposed. Rejecting would discard a relatively high-quality team.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Team [0,2] previously went on Quest 1 and succeeded with 0 fails, which makes 2 look good publicly while protecting 0. On Quest 2, team [0,2,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, which could implicate either 0 or 4 from the good players' perspective; 2 still looks relatively clean. Current phase is team voting. As evil, approving a 2-player team with my teammate 0 on it is strong because 0 can choose to pass or fail depending on deception needs, and including 2 maintains plausible deniability. My vote itself reveals little since many players may approve this seemingly reasonable team.

experiences.intentions: Player 1: I am Minion with evil teammate 0. Quest 2 failed on team [0,2,4], so at least one of 2/4 is likely good if 0 failed; quest 3 failed on [0,2], meaning either 0 or 2 failed, and since 0 is evil both failures can be explained by 0 alone. Thus 2 is plausibly good and publicly suspicious, while 3 and 4 are less tied to confirmed fails. We already have Evil at 2 wins, so the optimal play is to propose a team likely to be approved and containing at least one evil so we can secure the third fail. Including both 0 and me guarantees sabotage but may look too suspicious; however as leader on quest 4 I only need the team proposal. A team with 0 and 1 plus one more can still plausibly pass votes because 1 has not been on a failed quest yet. Information-wise, proposing both evils is aggressive, but if approved we can end the game immediately; if rejected, we still force others to react under pressure. Best chance to win now is maximize sabotage certainty.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, so team [0,1,3] contains both evils and guarantees we can fail quest 4 to win immediately. Previous fails on teams with 0 suggest I may already look suspicious, but at team voting the optimal evil play is to approve any team with enough evil control, especially one with both evils. Approving is consistent with wanting this team to go through; the information revealed by my vote matters less than securing the quest.
Player 3: I know 0 and 1 are evil from Merlin information. The proposed team [0,1,3] contains two known evils, so on quest 4 this is catastrophic for Good and should be rejected. As Merlin, I should avoid obviously perfect-seeming behavior over time, but rejecting a team with two visible evils is mandatory and consistent with any good player's reasoning given prior failed quests involving 0. My vote reveals some suspicion of 0/1, but approving would likely lose immediately.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,2,4] failed with 1 fail, strongly implicating 0 or one of 2/4; quest 3 team [0,2] failed with 1 fail, which essentially confirms 0 and/or 2, and since I know 0 is evil, 2 is likely good or at least not needed for evil. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [0,1,3]. As evil on quest 4 with evil already at 2 points, approving a team containing both evil players is optimal because if it passes, we can guarantee a fail and win immediately. My vote reveals some support for my own team, but that is natural and worth the chance to end the game.
Player 4: We know quest 1 on [0,2] failed, so at least one of 0/2 is evil. Quest 2 on [0,2,4] failed with me as 4 good, so again at least one of 0/2 is evil, consistent. Team [2,3,4] was rejected; if 3 were confidently good that team would have been attractive, but rejections are weak evidence. Current proposed team [0,1,3] includes 0, who is highly suspicious from being on both failed quests. At 1-2, good must reject risky teams and avoid likely evil. As a servant, my vote reveals suspicion of 0, which helps good coordination more than approving a likely losing team helps evil.
Player 2: I am Good with no hidden info. From results: Quest 1 [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 look relatively good. Quest 2 [0,2,4] had exactly one fail, so 4 is highly suspicious if 0 and I are good. Quest 3 [2,3,4] had exactly one fail; since I am good and 4 is already suspicious, 3 could still be good, but 4 remains strongest evil read. Proposed team [0,1,3] excludes both me and 4. Given 0 looks good, this team could be either all-good if 1 and 3 are good, or contain one evil among 1/3. At 1-2 down on Quest 4, Good must be careful, but this is a reasonable attempt to avoid the likely evil 4. Approving reveals willingness to test a team centered on the strongest good read and excluding the strongest evil read, which helps Good more than rejecting without a clearly better option.

On a 3-person team with both evil players 0 and 1 present, the optimal evil play is to sabotage now and end the game before assassination matters. My fail may reveal that team [0,1,3] contained evil, but that is irrelevant if the game ends with an evil quest victory this round.

Now quest 4 is do-or-die for good because evil already has 2 fails; as evil on the quest team with teammate 1 also present, the optimal play is to fail and immediately secure the third failed quest for evil.
